facade_colour: red brick rear wall with black-trim replacement windows and a small shed roof over the rear entry

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (56 Oxford Street) (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as part of a row, the properties have retained many of their original building features including their shared roofline with gables, brick cladding, segmentally arched window openings, and ground level bay windows. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential | Fine-grain Victorian rowhouses | Quiet

facade_description: 56 Oxford St presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 3 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as part of a row, the properties have retained many of their original building features including their shared roofline with gables, brick cladding, segmentally arched window openings, and ground level bay windows. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
